question:
For $i = 1,2$ let $T_i$ be a triangle with side lengths $a_i, b_i, c_i$,
and area $A_i$.  Suppose that $a_1 \le a_2,  b_1 \le b_2,  c_1 \le
c_2$,
and that $T_2$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $A_1
\le A_2$?
solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (partly due to Ravi Vakil)
Yes, it does follow.
For $i=1,2$,
let $P_i, Q_i, R_i$ be the vertices of $T_i$ opposide the sides of length
$a_i, b_i, c_i$, respectively.

We first check the case where $a_1 = a_2$ (or $b_1 = b_2$ or $c_1 = c_2$,
by the same argument after relabeling).
Imagine $T_2$ as being drawn with the base $Q_2R_2$
horizontal and the point $P_2$ above the line $Q_2R_2$. We may then
position $T_1$ so that $Q_1 = Q_2$, $R_1 = R_2$, and $P_1$ lies above the line
$Q_1R_1 = Q_2R_2$. Then $P_1$ also lies inside the region bounded by the
circles through $P_2$ centered at $Q_2$ and $R_2$. Since $\angle Q_2$
and $\angle R_2$ are acute, the part of this region above the line
$Q_2R_2$ lies within $T_2$. In particular, the
distance from $P_1$ to the line $Q_2R_2$ is less than or equal to the
distance from $P_2$ to the line $Q_2R_2$; hence $A_1 \leq A_2$.

To deduce the general case, put
\[
r = \max\{a_1/a_2, b_1/b_2, c_1/c_2\}.
\]
Let $T_3$ be the triangle with sides $ra_2, rb_2, rc_2$, which
has area $r^2 A_2$. Applying the special case to $T_1$ and $T_3$,
we deduce that $A_1 \leq r^2 A_2$; since $r \leq 1$ by hypothesis,
we have $A_1 \leq A_2$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:}
Another geometric argument in the case $a_1 = a_2$
is that since angles $\angle Q_2$ and $\angle R_2$
are acute, the perpendicular to $Q_2R_2$ through $P_2$ separates $Q_2$ from
$R_2$. If $A_1 > A_2$, then $P_1$ lies above the parallel to $Q_2R_2$
through $P_2$; if then it lies on or to the left of the vertical line
through $P_2$, we have $c_1 > c_2$ because the inequality holds
for both horizontal and vertical components (possibly with equality for one,
but not both). Similarly, if $P_1$ lies to the right of the vertical,
then $b_1 > b_2$.

\textbf{Second solution:} (attribution unknown)
Retain notation as in the first paragraph of the first solution.
Since the angle measures in any triangle add up to $\pi$, some angle
of $T_1$ must have measure less than or equal to its counterpart in
$T_2$. Without loss of generality assume that $\angle P_1 \leq \angle
P_2$. Since the latter is acute (because $T_2$ is acute), we have
$\sin \angle P_1 \leq \sin \angle P_2$. By the Law of Sines,
\[
A_1 = \frac{1}{2} b_1 c_1 \sin \angle P_1 \leq \frac{1}{2}
b_2 c_2 \sin \angle P_2 = A_2.
\]

\textbf{Remark:} Many other solutions are possible; for instance,
one uses Heron's formula for the area of a triangle in terms of
its side lengths.

descriptive_long.question:
For $indexvar = 1,2$ let $trianggen$ be a triangle with side lengths $sideagen, sidebgen, sidecgen$,
and area $areagen$.  Suppose that $sideaone \le sideatwo,  sidebone \le sidebtwo,  sidecone \le
sidectwo$,
and that $triangtwo$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $areaone
\le areatwo$?
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (partly due to Ravi Vakil)
Yes, it does follow.
For $indexvar=1,2$,
let $vertexpgen, vertexqgen, vertexrgen$ be the vertices of $trianggen$ opposide the sides of length
$sideagen, sidebgen, sidecgen$, respectively.

We first check the case where $sideaone = sideatwo$ (or $sidebone = sidebtwo$ or $sidecone = sidectwo$,
by the same argument after relabeling).
Imagine $triangtwo$ as being drawn with the base $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$
horizontal and the point $vertexptwo$ above the line $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$. We may then
position $triangone$ so that $vertexqone = vertexqtwo$, $vertexrone = vertexrtwo$, and $vertexpone$ lies above the line
$vertexqone vertexrone = vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$. Then $vertexpone$ also lies inside the region bounded by the
circles through $vertexptwo$ centered at $vertexqtwo$ and $vertexrtwo$. Since $\angle vertexqtwo$
and $\angle vertexrtwo$ are acute, the part of this region above the line
$vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$ lies within $triangtwo$. In particular, the
distance from $vertexpone$ to the line $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$ is less than or equal to the
distance from $vertexptwo$ to the line $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$; hence $areaone \leq areatwo$.

To deduce the general case, put
\[
scalerat = \max\{sideaone/sideatwo, sidebone/sidebtwo, sidecone/sidectwo\}.
\]
Let $triangthr$ be the triangle with sides $scalerat sideatwo, scalerat sidebtwo, scalerat sidectwo$, which
has area $scalerat^2 areatwo$. Applying the special case to $triangone$ and $triangthr$,
we deduce that $areaone \leq scalerat^2 areatwo$; since $scalerat \leq 1$ by hypothesis,
we have $areaone \leq areatwo$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:}
Another geometric argument in the case $sideaone = sideatwo$
is that since angles $\angle vertexqtwo$ and $\angle vertexrtwo$
are acute, the perpendicular to $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$ through $vertexptwo$ separates $vertexqtwo$ from
$vertexrtwo$. If $areaone > areatwo$, then $vertexpone$ lies above the parallel to $vertexqtwo vertexrtwo$
through $vertexptwo$; if then it lies on or to the left of the vertical line
through $vertexptwo$, we have $sidecone > sidectwo$ because the inequality holds
for both horizontal and vertical components (possibly with equality for one,
but not both). Similarly, if $vertexpone$ lies to the right of the vertical,
then $sidebone > sidebtwo$.

\textbf{Second solution:} (attribution unknown)
Retain notation as in the first paragraph of the first solution.
Since the angle measures in any triangle add up to $\pi$, some angle
of $triangone$ must have measure less than or equal to its counterpart in
$triangtwo$. Without loss of generality assume that $\angle vertexpone \leq \angle
vertexptwo$. Since the latter is acute (because $triangtwo$ is acute), we have
$\sin \angle vertexpone \leq \sin \angle vertexptwo$. By the Law of Sines,
\[
areaone = \frac{1}{2} sidebone sidecone \sin \angle vertexpone \leq \frac{1}{2}
sidebtwo sidectwo \sin \angle vertexptwo = areatwo.
\]

\textbf{Remark:} Many other solutions are possible; for instance,
one uses Heron's formula for the area of a triangle in terms of
its side lengths.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For $marigold = 1,2$ let $shipwreck$ be a triangle with side lengths $chandelier, honeycomb, raincloud$,
and area $driftwood$.  Suppose that $blueberry \le lemonade,  sandstone \le willowtree,  foxglove \le
riverbank$, and that $afterglow$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $paperclip
\le horseshoe$?
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (partly due to Ravi Vakil)
Yes, it does follow.
For $marigold=1,2$,
let $moonshine, falconry, zeppelin$ be the vertices of $shipwreck$ opposide the sides of length
$chandelier, honeycomb, raincloud$, respectively.

We first check the case where $blueberry = lemonade$ (or $sandstone = willowtree$ or $foxglove = riverbank$,
by the same argument after relabeling).
Imagine $afterglow$ as being drawn with the base $buttercupsunflower$
horizontal and the point $gemstone$ above the line $buttercupsunflower$. We may then
position $spicebush$ so that $blackberry = buttercup$, $arrowhead = sunflower$, and $starlight$ lies above the line
$blackberryarrowhead = buttercupsunflower$. Then $starlight$ also lies inside the region bounded by the
circles through $gemstone$ centered at $buttercup$ and $sunflower$. Since $\angle buttercup$
and $\angle sunflower$ are acute, the part of this region above the line
$buttercupsunflower$ lies within $afterglow$. In particular, the
distance from $starlight$ to the line $buttercupsunflower$ is less than or equal to the
distance from $gemstone$ to the line $buttercupsunflower$; hence $paperclip \leq horseshoe$.

To deduce the general case, put
\[
canarywood = \max\{blueberry/lemonade, sandstone/willowtree, foxglove/riverbank\}.
\]
Let $snowflake$ be the triangle with sides $canarywoodlemonade, canarywoodwillowtree, canarywoodriverbank$, which
has area $canarywood^2 horseshoe$. Applying the special case to $spicebush$ and $snowflake$,
we deduce that $paperclip \leq canarywood^2 horseshoe$; since $canarywood \leq 1$ by hypothesis,
we have $paperclip \leq horseshoe$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:}
Another geometric argument in the case $blueberry = lemonade$
is that since angles $\angle buttercup$ and $\angle sunflower$
are acute, the perpendicular to $buttercupsunflower$ through $gemstone$ separates $buttercup$ from
$sunflower$. If $paperclip > horseshoe$, then $starlight$ lies above the parallel to $buttercupsunflower$
through $gemstone$; if then it lies on or to the left of the vertical line
through $gemstone$, we have $foxglove > riverbank$ because the inequality holds
for both horizontal and vertical components (possibly with equality for one,
but not both). Similarly, if $starlight$ lies to the right of the vertical,
then $sandstone > willowtree$.

\textbf{Second solution:} (attribution unknown)
Retain notation as in the first paragraph of the first solution.
Since the angle measures in any triangle add up to $\pi$, some angle
of $spicebush$ must have measure less than or equal to its counterpart in
$afterglow$. Without loss of generality assume that $\angle starlight \leq \angle
gemstone$. Since the latter is acute (because $afterglow$ is acute), we have
$\sin \angle starlight \leq \sin \angle gemstone$. By the Law of Sines,
\[
paperclip = \frac{1}{2} sandstone foxglove \sin \angle starlight \leq \frac{1}{2}
willowtree riverbank \sin \angle gemstone = horseshoe.
\]

\textbf{Remark:} Many other solutions are possible; for instance,
one uses Heron's formula for the area of a triangle in terms of
its side lengths.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For $collective = 1,2$ let $smalltrianglevar$ be a triangle with side lengths $shortedgevar, narrowedgevar, tinyedgevar$,
and area $smallareavar$.  Suppose that $shortedgeone \le shortedgetwo,  narrowedgeone \le narrowedgetwo,  tinyedgeone \le
tinyedgetwo$, and that $smalltriangletwo$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $smallareaone
\le smallareatwo$?
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (partly due to Ravi Vakil)
Yes, it does follow.
For $collective=1,2$,
let $farvertexvar, innervertexvar, outervertexvar$ be the vertices of $smalltrianglevar$ opposide the sides of length
$shortedgevar, narrowedgevar, tinyedgevar$, respectively.

We first check the case where $shortedgeone = shortedgetwo$ (or $narrowedgeone = narrowedgetwo$ or $tinyedgeone = tinyedgetwo$,
by the same argument after relabeling).
Imagine $smalltriangletwo$ as being drawn with the base $innervertextwo outervertextwo$
horizontal and the point $farvertextwo$ above the line $innervertextwo outervertextwo$. We may then
position $smalltriangleone$ so that $innervertexone = innervertextwo$, $outervertexone = outervertextwo$, and $farvertexone$ lies above the line
$innervertexone outervertexone = innervertextwo outervertextwo$. Then $farvertexone$ also lies inside the region bounded by the
circles through $farvertextwo$ centered at $innervertextwo$ and $outervertextwo$. Since $\angle innervertextwo$
and $\angle outervertextwo$ are acute, the part of this region above the line
$innervertextwo outervertextwo$ lies within $smalltriangletwo$. In particular, the
distance from $farvertexone$ to the line $innervertextwo outervertextwo$ is less than or equal to the
distance from $farvertextwo$ to the line $innervertextwo outervertextwo$; hence $smallareaone \leq smallareatwo$.

To deduce the general case, put
\[
expansion = \max\{shortedgeone/shortedgetwo, narrowedgeone/narrowedgetwo, tinyedgeone/tinyedgetwo\}.
\]
Let $smalltriangletri$ be the triangle with sides $expansionshortedgetwo, expansionnarrowedgetwo, expansiontinyedgetwo$, which
has area $expansion^2 smallareatwo$. Applying the special case to $smalltriangleone$ and $smalltriangletri$,
we deduce that $smallareaone \leq expansion^2 smallareatwo$; since $expansion \leq 1$ by hypothesis,
we have $smallareaone \leq smallareatwo$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:}
Another geometric argument in the case $shortedgeone = shortedgetwo$
is that since angles $\angle innervertextwo$ and $\angle outervertextwo$
are acute, the perpendicular to $innervertextwo outervertextwo$ through $farvertextwo$ separates $innervertextwo$ from
outervertextwo. If $smallareaone > smallareatwo$, then $farvertexone$ lies above the parallel to $innervertextwo outervertextwo$
through $farvertextwo$; if then it lies on or to the left of the vertical line
through $farvertextwo$, we have $tinyedgeone > tinyedgetwo$ because the inequality holds
for both horizontal and vertical components (possibly with equality for one,
but not both). Similarly, if $farvertexone$ lies to the right of the vertical,
then $narrowedgeone > narrowedgetwo$.

\textbf{Second solution:} (attribution unknown)
Retain notation as in the first paragraph of the first solution.
Since the angle measures in any triangle add up to $\pi$, some angle
of $smalltriangleone$ must have measure less than or equal to its counterpart in
$smalltriangletwo$. Without loss of generality assume that $\angle farvertexone \leq \angle
farvertextwo$. Since the latter is acute (because $smalltriangletwo$ is acute), we have
$\sin \angle farvertexone \leq \sin \angle farvertextwo$. By the Law of Sines,
\[
smallareaone = \frac{1}{2} narrowedgeone tinyedgeone \sin \angle farvertexone \leq \frac{1}{2}
narrowedgetwo tinyedgetwo \sin \angle farvertextwo = smallareatwo.
\]

\textbf{Remark:} Many other solutions are possible; for instance,
one uses Heron's formula for the area of a triangle in terms of
its side lengths.

garbled_string.question: For $i = 1,2$ let $efsatznb$ be a triangle with side lengths $wqbrtusk, lzhxsqaf, sbheculp$, and area $hwcfkuei$.  Suppose that $ygrldpma \le czmhvexo,  uvnkysop \le jdfkreli,  qpelavrn \le tmgoswzb$, and that $oquzjwen$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $gpntzyor \le xdqsmbev$?
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:} (partly due to Ravi Vakil)
Yes, it does follow.
For $i=1,2$,
let $dwtpghyu, fyumdoqe, vtslqahn$ be the vertices of $efsatznb$ opposide the sides of length
$wqbrtusk, lzhxsqaf, sbheculp$, respectively.

We first check the case where $ygrldpma = czmhvexo$ (or $uvnkysop = jdfkreli$ or $qpelavrn = tmgoswzb$,
by the same argument after relabeling).
Imagine $oquzjwen$ as being drawn with the base $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$
horizontal and the point $bldmxnqe$ above the line $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$. We may then
position $imldvrcg$ so that $zkelhpmf = nrcbvasj$, $xoygkrpl = kjdfercu$, and $abjvcsor$ lies above the line
$zkelhpmfxoygkrpl = nrcbvasjkjdfercu$. Then $abjvcsor$ also lies inside the region bounded by the
circles through $bldmxnqe$ centered at $nrcbvasj$ and $kjdfercu$. Since $\angle nrcbvasj$
and $\angle kjdfercu$ are acute, the part of this region above the line
$nrcbvasjkjdfercu$ lies within $oquzjwen$. In particular, the
distance from $abjvcsor$ to the line $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$ is less than or equal to the
distance from $bldmxnqe$ to the line $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$; hence $gpntzyor \leq xdqsmbev$.

To deduce the general case, put
\[
kxvpzndq = \max\{ygrldpma/czmhvexo, uvnkysop/jdfkreli, qpelavrn/tmgoswzb\}.
\]
Let $gprkdwha$ be the triangle with sides $kxvpzndq czmhvexo, kxvpzndq jdfkreli, kxvpzndq tmgoswzb$, which
has area $kxvpzndq^2 xdqsmbev$. Applying the special case to $imldvrcg$ and $gprkdwha$,
we deduce that $gpntzyor \leq kxvpzndq^2 xdqsmbev$; since $kxvpzndq \leq 1$ by hypothesis,
we have $gpntzyor \leq xdqsmbev$ as desired.

\textbf{Remark:}
Another geometric argument in the case $ygrldpma = czmhvexo$
is that since angles $\angle nrcbvasj$ and $\angle kjdfercu$
are acute, the perpendicular to $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$ through $bldmxnqe$ separates $nrcbvasj$ from
$kjdfercu$. If $gpntzyor > xdqsmbev$, then $abjvcsor$ lies above the parallel to $nrcbvasjkjdfercu$
through $bldmxnqe$; if then it lies on or to the left of the vertical line
through $bldmxnqe$, we have $qpelavrn > tmgoswzb$ because the inequality holds
for both horizontal and vertical components (possibly with equality for one,
but not both). Similarly, if $abjvcsor$ lies to the right of the vertical,
then $uvnkysop > jdfkreli$.

\textbf{Second solution:} (attribution unknown)
Retain notation as in the first paragraph of the first solution.
Since the angle measures in any triangle add up to $\pi$, some angle
of $imldvrcg$ must have measure less than or equal to its counterpart in
$oquzjwen$. Without loss of generality assume that $\angle abjvcsor \leq \angle
bldmxnqe$. Since the latter is acute (because $oquzjwen$ is acute), we have
$\sin \angle abjvcsor \leq \sin \angle bldmxnqe$. By the Law of Sines,
\[
gpntzyor = \frac{1}{2} uvnkysop qpelavrn \sin \angle abjvcsor \leq \frac{1}{2}
jdfkreli tmgoswzb \sin \angle bldmxnqe = xdqsmbev.
\]

\textbf{Remark:} Many other solutions are possible; for instance,
one uses Heron's formula for the area of a triangle in terms of
its side lengths.

kernel_variant.question:
Let \Delta X and \Delta Y be two triangles.  In \Delta X label the vertices L_X , M_X , N_X and let the side lengths opposite them be \ell _X , m_X , n_X , respectively.  Label \Delta Y analogously, so its sides opposite L_Y , M_Y , N_Y are \ell _Y , m_Y , n_Y.  Assume

1.  \ell _X \leq  \ell _Y , m_X \leq  m_Y , n_X \leq  n_Y  (each side of \Delta X is not longer than the corresponding side of \Delta Y);
2.  every angle of \Delta Y is at most 90^\circ (i.e. \Delta Y is non-obtuse).

Let K_X and K_Y denote the areas of \Delta X and \Delta Y, respectively.  Prove that one always has

            K_X \leq  K_Y.
kernel_variant.solution:
Because the three angles in any triangle add up to \pi , at least one angle of \Delta X cannot exceed the corresponding angle of \Delta Y.  Choose such a vertex and call it V (so V is one of L, M, N and

            \angle V_X  \leq   \angle V_Y.      (1)

Since \Delta Y is non-obtuse, \angle V_Y \leq  \pi /2, and therefore \angle V_X \leq  \angle V_Y \leq  \pi /2.  Consequently both angles lie in the interval [0, \pi /2], on which the sine function is increasing; hence

            sin\angle V_X  \leq   sin\angle V_Y.      (2)

Let the two sides of a triangle that meet at vertex V be denoted by p_\sigma  and q_\sigma  (\sigma  = X, Y).  Because every corresponding pair of sides satisfies the component-wise inequalities in the hypothesis, we have

            p_X \leq  p_Y   and   q_X \leq  q_Y.      (3)

For each triangle, the area can be expressed using the two sides adjacent to V and the included angle at V:

            K_X = \frac{1}{2} \cdot  p_X \cdot  q_X \cdot  sin\angle V_X,
            K_Y = \frac{1}{2} \cdot  p_Y \cdot  q_Y \cdot  sin\angle V_Y.      (4)

Combining (2), (3) and (4) gives

            K_X = \frac{1}{2} \cdot  p_X \cdot  q_X \cdot  sin\angle V_X
                 \leq  \frac{1}{2} \cdot  p_Y \cdot  q_Y \cdot  sin\angle V_Y = K_Y,

which is the desired inequality.

Therefore, if each side of \Delta X is no longer than the corresponding side of a non-obtuse triangle \Delta Y, then the area of \Delta X does not exceed the area of \Delta Y.

core_steps: By angle-sum, at least one angle of T₁ is ≤ its counterpart in T₂.
Since T₂ is (at least) non-obtuse, both chosen angles lie in [0,π/2], so sine is monotone and sin θ₁ ≤ sin θ₂.
Write the areas as A = (1/2)(product of the two sides adjacent to the chosen angle)·sin θ.
Combine side inequalities (those two sides) with sin θ₁ ≤ sin θ₂ to get A₁ ≤ A₂.

slot1.description: Names of vertices and opposite sides may be relabeled arbitrarily.

slot2.description: Which specific angle (and its two adjacent sides) is selected for the comparison.
slot2.original: Angle at vertex P with adjacent sides b and c

slot3.description: Full set of side inequalities can be weakened to just the two sides adjacent to the selected angle.
slot3.original: a₁ ≤ a₂, b₁ ≤ b₂, c₁ ≤ c₂

slot4.description: ‘Acute’ can be broadened to ‘non-obtuse’; monotonicity of sine still holds.

slot5.description: Indices 1 and 2 that distinguish the triangles may be replaced by any two distinct labels.
